turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Right, that's an arrest warrant.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: -- in order to enter the house. Not that it requires that --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: So the -- so that -- so the warrant saying really doesn't protect the house then, it protects -- it really is a protection of him personally.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: It's a protection of the house insofar as the arrest is to be made there.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: It's a protection of him --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well, I guess --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- if he's in his house.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: I'll put it this way. You submit this on your brief, your position is on here, you agree with the position you've stated in your brief?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: Do you think a warrant is in anyway infirm that it does no more than give the name of the person to be arrested without any address, without any more?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: That would depend what kind of a warrant --

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: An arrest warrant, an arrest warrant.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: I would say not necessarily if --

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: Not -- well, yes or no under the federal constitution, that's all we're talking about.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: Under the federal constitution, I do not think. I think there have to be a premises identified.

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: What, premises of the person to be arrested?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Mr. Hellerstein --

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: -- extraordinary thing.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Whether it says John Smith and address unknown --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Do you need a new --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: John Smith address unknown and his address is unknown.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: If that was --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: That there's plenty of reason to --

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: If this Court had held --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- there's a probable cause to believe that John Smith committed a criminal offense.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: If this Court had held that the warrant was required so far as the probable cause to believe commission of the crime, I think it would be sufficient, yes.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: What --

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: And then the officers locating the premises could go in without warrant.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Help me Mr. Hellerstein, I have never seen a warrant that said what you said, that this is a warrant for the arrest of John Doe, and if you find him in his house, you're going to arrest him there.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: There are --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: I have never seen one like that.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: There are John Doe warrants Mr. Justice.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: This isn't John -- I mean this is James Smith.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: A real one.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: This is, what's his name, Theodore Payton, and if you find him at home, you can arrest him at home. I've never seen a warrant like that.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: Neither have I.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: I don't know what --

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: I thought you said the warrant had to say that they can arrest him at home?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: No, I think -- what I meant to say is that the warrant had to be obtained in order for there to be an arrest within the home.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, it doesn't have to say so in the warrant.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: He can be arrested in a public place without a warrant. He can be arrested in his or somebody else's home only with a warrant --

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: That is my --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- an arrest warrant.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Let me be -- probably repeating but it seems to me there'd been some misunderstanding. In this very case, talking about Mr. Payton, as I understand the sequence of events they'd learned his identity at one time and his name at another time and his address at another time. If at the time before they knew his address, they knew his identity and a probable cause to believe he was responsible for the offense and they got an arrest warrant for that person without naming an address. They had then taken that warrant, later learned his address and broken down the door as they did in this case. Would you concede they acted within the Constitution?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: If they had the arrest warrant?

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Yes, but with no address on it.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: I would be reluctant to concede that Mr. Justice Stevens, because I've had no experience with that context. I think that I might go so far as to say that mainly because the warrant had been obtained by a magistrate who had made the determination that there was new probable cause. And once the determination was made by a magistrate --

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Would you think there may be a constitutional difference between a warrant that includes the man's address and one that does not? That's the delay.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Where whom?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: I don't think there was a difference.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Well, otherwise you should concede that because you have conceded, if I understand you correctly, that if the warrant includes his address that's all that's necessary. They don't know where he is.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: They get a warrant to get to his -- to arrest this man. They know where he lives. They go out the first place they look is in his home and nobody answers the door so they break it down. You say that's all right, but it's not all right if they don't know his address before they get the warrant.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Is that your position?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: No, I think that in terms of what the constitution required a warrant --

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: That's what we're talking about here.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: -- it should have the man's address in it.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Well, you're saying then that the -- there's a constitutional difference depending on whether they get his address in the warrant or not?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: I'm not certain of that though. I'm not certain that is -- I think the key thing is that the warrant --

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: It seems to me that if you take that position you are really agreeing with Mr. Justice White that you want some kind of constitutional protection on knowing whether he's in the house or not, and that would be more like a search warrant. And then you're going to say the time you go in to get the warrant, you got to be able to predict whether he'd be in the house at the time they get there or not.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: Well, that's not what I'm after. I'm after the determination that a magistrate makes with respect to prior -- to going to the home. I don't know what the experience would be or in the Constitution --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well, if your big worry is having the magistrate's confirmation as to probable cause to -- that this man committed a crime, I would think you would like to urge that just generally that there just should be arrest warrants at anytime whenever you think at anytime you get them.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: Mr. Justice, I can --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: And not just for the house if there'd be -- if aim of this protection as to -- is not protected -- if it isn't aimed at protecting the privacy of the home but just probable cause to commit with respect to the commission of the crime. That's general applicability.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: After the Court's decision in Watson, I can urge that. What I'm saying --

turns.name: BYRON R. WHITE
turns.text: Well, you might be back though another day, do you?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: I think it'd be a while but I believe that Watson says is not required. What I'm trying simply to say is that a warrant to enter the home is required beyond Watson. That is -- I'm not seeking a warrant for arrest. I'm seeking for a warrant for arrest within the home.

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: Let me see if I understand the net of your argument. Is it this that whenever there is an arrest, it can be made without a warrant only if they're arrested in a public place? If they are arrested anywhere else, they must have a warrant?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: No, Mr. Chief Justice. I think that states it far too --

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: Well, the pieces of your argument add up to that for me.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: No, I'm simply saying that there can be an arrest -- there can be no arrest within the home without a warrant.

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: How about his office?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: An office? Depending on the nature of the office and the extent --

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: Well, where does the constitution help you there, that's what we're guided by.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: Just in the continuum of this Court's -- that's not this case. This Court has another decisions, afforded offices some protection under the Fourth Amendment depending on what the expectations of privacy were, who had access to the office. I would be willing to concede that an office that was open to the public to do business was not in any way like the home. And if that's not --

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: The lawyer's office, the private office.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: I think the interior of my office where I might work alone would have the same privacy of the home, but not necessarily where I see clients and do business. That would be I would draw the line.

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: Some of the members of the Court would like to have an opportunity to examine those copies of bench warrants or grand jury warrants if your friend has no objection, would you leave them?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: I'd be most happy to leave them Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: If you'll deposit them with the clerk.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. HELLERSTEIN
turns.text: I will do that.

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: Thank you. Thank you gentlemen. The case is submitted.
